title: Open-Weight LLMs in 2026
subtitle: Who leads on benchmarks, licensing, and adoption

figures.title: How this report was built
figures.caption: Figure 1. A multi-agent research society over a shared blackboard: the topic is decomposed into gaps, researchers fill them from the web, a critic re-bands coverage and reopens weak gaps, and a synthesizer writes the cited report once coverage is rich.

figures.title: Coding — SWE-bench Verified
figures.caption: Figure 2. Open-weight coding leaders on SWE-bench Verified. DeepSeek V4 Pro leads; all six are Chinese labs. Sources: model cards, llm-stats.com.

figures.title: Reasoning & math
figures.caption: Figure 3. Reasoning & math (AIME 2025, GPQA Diamond). GLM-5.2 separately leads knowledge at 91.2% GPQA. Sources: huggingface.co model cards, llm-stats.com.

figures.title: License permissiveness by family
figures.caption: Figure 4. Licensing permissiveness — greener is more permissive. The Chinese frontier consolidated on Apache-2.0/MIT; Llama and Gemma stay restricted. Sources: huggingface.co, ai.google.dev/gemma/terms.

figures.title: OpenRouter cumulative tokens by author
figures.caption: Figure 5. Adoption: DeepSeek served more OpenRouter tokens than Qwen, Llama and Mistral combined. Source: OpenRouter State of AI, arxiv.org/abs/2601.10088.

figures.title: The China-vs-West usage shift
figures.caption: Figure 6. Usage flipped toward China: US-origin OpenRouter token share fell ~70% → ~30% while Chinese-origin rose to ~46% (contested 46–51%). Sources: OpenRouter trackers.
